topic: Why The Ozone Hole Lets More UV Through
title: How Chlorine Chemistry Weakens Earth's Stratospheric Shield

lesson.learn: The ozone hole is a seasonal thinning of the stratospheric ozone layer, most famously observed over Antarctica. Ozone absorbs much of the Sun's harmful ultraviolet radiation, so when ozone levels fall, more UV reaches the lower atmosphere and the ground. The main human cause was chlorofluorocarbons, or CFCs, which release chlorine in the stratosphere and trigger catalytic ozone loss. The problem became a major scientific and policy issue because it showed that a thin layer high above Earth can have a direct impact on life below.
lesson.simpler: The ozone layer works like Earth's UV shield. When chemicals break down that shield, more harmful sunlight can get through.
lesson.keyTakeaway: The ozone hole formed because human-made chemicals destroyed ozone in the stratosphere, but international action helped slow the damage and begin recovery.
lesson.deeperDive: CFCs were attractive industrial chemicals because they were stable, nonflammable, and useful in refrigerants and sprays. That stability was also the problem: they drifted into the stratosphere, where ultraviolet light could break them apart and release reactive chlorine. One chlorine atom can destroy many ozone molecules through a catalytic cycle before being removed from the atmosphere. Over Antarctica, the polar vortex isolates cold air and creates polar stratospheric clouds, which speed up the chemistry that destroys ozone each spring. The Montreal Protocol of 1987 is one of the clearest examples of global environmental cooperation, because phasing out ozone-depleting substances helped the ozone layer begin a slow recovery.
lesson.funFact: The first major Antarctic ozone hole was not discovered by a satellite. It was identified from ground-based measurements by scientists working in Antarctica.

people: Joe Farman - first Antarctic ozone hole observer
F. Sherwood Rowland - CFC and ozone research
places: Antarctica - where the ozone hole is most dramatic
The stratosphere - ozone-rich layer of the atmosphere
Halley Research Station - key observation site
NASA and NOAA ozone monitoring centers
years: 2000 - one of the largest ozone holes recorded
